The Strait of Hormuz is more than an oil route; it's a global lifeline that, when blocked, triggers a domino effect across every aspect of modern life.

Food Scarcity: It controls nearly half the world’s fertilizer exports. Blockages mean higher prices and lower crop yields, leading to global hunger.

Medical & Tech Crisis: We lose the by-products needed for MRI machines, semiconductors, and essential packaging.

The Green Irony: Even building wind turbines and electric cars requires the specific petroleum by-products trapped in the Gulf.

The Bottom Line: We aren't just facing a fuel shortage; we are facing a shortage of the "invisible" ingredients that keep the world fed, healthy, and moving.

**MACQUARIE ON INDIA EV: ADOPTION RISING, NEW LAUNCHES DRIVING MOMENTUM**

**CORE HIGHLIGHT**  
• BEV penetration steadily increasing in passenger vehicles  
• Premium segment expansion + new launches accelerating adoption  

**BEV PENETRATION TREND**  
• PV BEV penetration: 2.4% (CY24) → 4% (CY25)  
• Further improved to ~4–5% in Q1 CY26  
• Indicates gradual but consistent EV adoption  

**SEGMENT SHIFT (₹20–30 LAKH BAND)**  
• Share increased from 9% (CY24) → 24% (CY25) → 36% (Q1 CY26)  
• Premium EV segment seeing strong traction  
• Driven by better product offerings & features  

**KEY DRIVERS**  
• Multiple new launches in ₹20–30 lakh segment  
• 3 models from M&M  
• 1 model each from Tata Motors, Hyundai, and Maruti  
• Bigger battery packs addressing range anxiety concerns  

**INDUSTRY CHALLENGES**  
• Charging infrastructure ramp-up remains a key bottleneck  
• Limited EV-specific models; reliance on ICE-converted platforms  
• Availability constraints impacting adoption pace  

**OUTLOOK (12–24 MONTHS)**  
• BEV adoption expected to gradually rise  
• Improved product mix + infrastructure development key triggers  

**COMPANY VIEW**  
• M&M: Best positioned among PV peers in BEV space  
• Strong pipeline of EV launches supports growth visibility  
• Target Price: ₹4,168  

**2W EV SEGMENT**  
• TVS Motor strengthening EV leadership  
• Recent launch in sub-₹1 lakh segment expands accessibility  

**RISKS**  
• Weak demand environment  
• Commodity price-led margin pressure  
• Intensifying competition in EV space  

**MARKET STRATEGY (AAJ / KAL)**  
• Auto EV players: Positive bias (especially M&M, TVS)  
• Watch execution of new EV launches  
• Infra development remains key monitorable  

**KEY WATCHPOINTS**  
• EV charging infrastructure expansion  
• New EV model pipeline & acceptance  
• Battery cost trends  
• Competitive intensity  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive – Structural EV adoption trend intact, led by premium segment expansion and new launches

**JEFFERIES ON CEMENT: COST PRESSURE BUILDS, PRICE HIKES LIKELY AHEAD**

**CORE HIGHLIGHT**  
• Cement prices largely stable MoM in March  
• Modest improvement of +1.5% QoQ in Q4FY26  
• Strong volume growth trend continues  

**DEMAND & VOLUME TRENDS**  
• Q4FY26 volume growth remains healthy  
• Builds on ~9% YoY growth seen in 3QFY26  
• Demand environment supportive across regions  

**COST PRESSURES**  
• Cost per tonne increased sharply by ~₹300/Tn  
• International coal prices ↑ 18% QoQ  
• US petcoke prices ↑ 12% QoQ  
• Packing costs surged 40–50% due to material shortages  

**PRICING DYNAMICS**  
• Industry needs ~5–7% price hike (₹20–25 per bag)  
• Required to offset rising input costs  
• Dealers indicate attempts of ₹15–50 per bag hikes in April  

**INDUSTRY ACTION SIGNALS**  
• Price hikes expected in April–May period  
• Industry pushing aggressively to pass on cost inflation  
• Success of hikes remains key monitorable  

**MARGIN OUTLOOK**  
• Margins under pressure due to cost spike  
• Recovery dependent on successful price hikes  
• Near-term profitability remains sensitive to input costs  

**MARKET STRATEGY (AAJ / KAL)**  
• Cement stocks: Watch for price hike execution  
• Cost inflation remains key overhang  
• Volume growth supportive but margins at risk  

**KEY WATCHPOINTS**  
• Success of April–May price hikes  
• Trend in coal & petcoke prices  
• Packing material cost normalization  
• Demand momentum sustainability  

**IMPACT**  
• Neutral to Slightly Negative – Strong demand but rising costs may pressure margins unless price hikes sustain

🚨 India’s $4 Billion MRO Opportunity: From Dependency to Strategic Control

India has long relied on foreign countries for maintaining its defence platforms — with 80–90% of MRO work done overseas.

That is now changing.

📌 What is MRO & Why It Matters?
MRO (Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul) ensures operational readiness of aircraft, ships, and submarines.

⚠️ Without domestic MRO:
– Delays in repairs
– High costs
– Strategic vulnerability in conflict

📊 Massive Demand Build-Up

India’s fleet expansion is driving demand:

✈️ IAF: ~1,800 aircraft by 2030
🚢 Navy: 200 warships, submarines doubling
🛫 Civil aviation: ~1,500 aircraft

📈 MRO market:
– $1.2B (2025) → $4B by 2030
– 27% CAGR
– Defence = 60% of growth

💰 Policy Push (Budget 2026)

₹5,000 Cr dedicated MRO package:

✔️ 100% tax holiday (5 yrs)
✔️ GST exemptions on spares
✔️ 150% depreciation on capex
✔️ 60% localization mandate in defence MRO
✔️ ₹12,000 Cr+ FDI commitments (GE, Safran, Honeywell)

🏗️ Infrastructure is Now Real

🔹 Cochin Shipyard mega dock operational
🔹 Mazagon Dock & Hindustan Shipyard expanding
🔹 Engine MRO by Rolls Royce, GE, Tata JV
🔹 HAL composite repair capabilities

👉 India is building full-stack MRO infra across air & naval segments

🌍 Big Strategic Shifts (2026)

✔️ Karwar submarine test facility (global exclusivity)
✔️ Vietnam submarine refit deal → entry into $2B regional market
✔️ Indo-Pacific Refit Alliance (QUAD-linked opportunity)

⚙️ Economics Are Attractive

💸 ₹15,000 Cr annual forex savings potential
💰 Engine overhaul: ₹50 Cr per visit
🚢 Carrier refit: ₹1,000 Cr

📉 AI + digital twins → 30% lifecycle cost reduction

🧩 Challenges Ahead

⚠️ OEM certifications (especially submarines)
⚠️ Skilled workforce gap (1.2M needed by 2030)
⚠️ Fragmented state-level ecosystem
⚠️ Trust-building for classified defence work

📌 Final Insight

India has moved from intent → execution:

✔️ Policy ready
✔️ Infra operational
✔️ FDI committed
✔️ First export contracts signed

👉 The real test now:
Can India build trust and become the Indo-Pacific’s MRO hub?

MID & SMALL CAPS: STRUCTURAL RE-RATING vs CYCLICAL OUTPERFORMANCE

CORE OBSERVATION  
• Mid & Small caps outperforming Nifty even during stress phases  
• Rare historically → typically they underperform in crises  
• Outperformance sustained across 1Y / 2Y / trailing periods  

WHAT’S DIFFERENT THIS TIME  
• Balance sheets:  
  – Lower leverage vs pre-COVID  
  – Reduced interest burden  
• Cash flows:  
  – Better working capital discipline  
  – Higher FCF conversion  
• Profitability:  
  – Operating leverage post demand recovery  
• Corporate governance:  
  – Cleaner businesses vs earlier cycles  

STRUCTURAL DRIVERS  
• China+1 + manufacturing shift → benefits mid-cap exporters  
• PLI schemes → capex cycle revival in mid-sized companies  
• Formalisation of economy → market share gains from unorganised  
• Domestic demand resilience (India-specific growth story)  

WHY THEY OUTPERFORMED EVEN IN “STRESS”  
• This is NOT a balance-sheet crisis (like 2008 / 2013 / 2018 IL&FS)  
• Liquidity still reasonably strong in the system  
• Earnings downgrades limited vs large caps (banks/commodities earlier cycles)  
• Retail + SIP flows structurally supporting broader markets  

VALUATION REALITY CHECK  
• Re-rating justified partly by:  
  – Better quality  
  – Higher growth visibility  
• But:  
  – Many pockets already pricing perfection  
  – Dispersion very high (winners vs junk)  

WHERE YOU ARE RIGHT  
• Earnings growth differential likely higher vs large caps  
• True multi-baggers historically emerge from this segment  
• Quality today is far superior vs pre-COVID  

WHERE CAUTION IS NEEDED  
• Mean reversion still applies (even in structural stories)  
• Liquidity cycles can reverse faster than fundamentals  
• Not all mid/small caps = quality (selection risk very high)  
• Valuation corrections may not be deep like past crashes, but drawdowns (20–40%) are still normal  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Structural bull case intact (India growth + corporate health)  
• But returns will be:  
  – More stock-specific (not index-wide)  
  – More volatile than large caps  

WHAT TO FOCUS ON (CRITICAL)  
• Earnings consistency (not just growth)  
• Cash flow > reported profits  
• Capital allocation discipline  
• Avoid leverage + story-driven narratives  

BOTTOM LINE  
• You’re likely in a structural re-rating phase, not just a cyclical rally  
• Biggest winners will come from mid/small caps — but only high-quality, scalable businesses  
• Expect volatility, not a straight line up

• Strong FCF generation expected from FY28 onward

• Discount factors: Late cell entry + lower integration + execution risk

**IMPACT**  
• Positive (High Growth, Execution-Heavy) – Strong earnings inflection story, but success hinges on timely integration and margin recovery

**MSUMI: OPERATING LEVERAGE + CONTENT GROWTH + EV OPTIONALITY STORY**

**CORE THESIS**  
• Business model: High operating leverage + rising content per vehicle  
• Revenue CAGR: ~13% (FY26–28) driven by PV, 2W and EV growth  
• Earnings CAGR: ~27% with strong margin expansion  
• Greenfield ramp-up: Key driver of profitability and RoCE improvement  

**KEY GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• Greenfield utilization: ~45% → 65–70% (FY27) → further upside in FY28  
• Content growth:  
  – PV: ~1.7x increase (EV + premiumisation)  
  – 2W: ~3x increase with EV transition  
• Segment growth: PV & 2W outperform CV and agri/off-road  
• Strong OEM pipeline visibility due to dominant market position  

**STRUCTURAL EDGE (MOAT)**  
• Market leadership: >55% PV harness share, >25% in 2W  
• High customer stickiness with OEM integration  
• Sumitomo partnership → access to advanced systems (ECUs, junction boxes, EV solutions)  
• 30 manufacturing facilities with fungible capacity (flexibility across programs)  

**VALUE CREATION MODEL**  
• Operating leverage from utilization ramp-up  
• Content per vehicle expansion → structural revenue growth  
• Mix improvement (premium vehicles + EVs) boosts margins  
• Automation + disciplined capex enhances RoIC  

**OPTIONALITY (HIDDEN VALUE)**  
• EV transition: Multi-year structural content increase  
• Premiumisation (shift to UVs): ~1.5x content uplift in PVs  
• Advanced electronics integration → move up value chain (beyond wiring harness)  

**FINANCIAL TRAJECTORY**  
• Revenue: ₹111.7bn → ₹143.0bn (FY26–28)  
• EBITDA: ₹10.6bn → ₹16.1bn (~20–26% growth)  
• EBITDA margin: 9.5% → 11.3%  
• PAT: ₹6.1bn → ₹9.9bn  
• EPS growth: 23.7% (FY27E), 31.0% (FY28E)  
• RoE: 41–50% | RoCE: 49–60% (best-in-class returns)  

**BUSINESS MIX & OPERATING LEVERAGE**  
• Revenue mix:  
  – PV ~62% (core driver)  
  – 2W ~13–14% (fastest growth)  
  – CV ~9–10%, Agri ~6%, Others ~8–9%  
• Key plants: Navagam (Gujarat), Pune, Kharkhoda → utilization ramp critical  
• Capex: ~₹2bn annually with strong FCF (₹5.1bn → ₹9.3bn)  
• High payout ratio (~85%) supported by strong cash flows  

**COST STRUCTURE & RISKS (INPUT SIDE)**  
• Gross margin stable: ~33–34%  
• Cost mix: Employee ~17–18%, Other expenses ~6–7%  
• Copper exposure: ~10% of revenue  
  – Pass-through: 60–70% (3 months), balance (6 months lag)  
• Imports: ~27–28% of RM (limited near-term disruption risk)  

**VALUATION**  
• P/E: 41x / 33x / 25x (FY26E / FY27E / FY28E)  
• EV/EBITDA: 23.9x → 15.6x (compression with growth)  
• Target Price: ₹60 (40x FY28E EPS)  
• Premium justified by:  
  – High visibility growth  
  – Strong RoE/RoCE  
  – Structural EV tailwinds  

**KEY RISKS**  
• Weak domestic auto demand  
• Competitive intensity (global players like Aptiv, Yazaki)

Buying the Future, Not Nostalgia

​The attached article serves as a sobering "reality check" for investors who equate a company's past glory with its future returns. While giants like Asian Paints, HUL, and TCS remain fundamentally strong businesses, the data from 2019 to 2026 highlights a critical lesson: valuation acts as gravity. When quality stocks are bid up to "priced-for-perfection" levels—sometimes exceeding 70x or 80x earnings—even solid profit growth can result in "lost decades" or paltry returns as multiples inevitably compress.

​The shift from the 2009–2019 "golden era" to the current slowdown proves that a great company can be a poor investment if the entry price ignores a margin of safety. As interest rates normalized and liquidity tightened, the market stopped paying for "nostalgia" and began demanding tangible earnings acceleration to justify high premiums. For the modern investor, the takeaway is clear: dominance in the market does not guarantee dominance in your portfolio if you overpay for the privilege of owning it.

reports.text:
Jal Jeevan Mission has enabled massive scale-up. Last-mile challenges remain

India has pulled off a quiet transformation under the mission with tap water coverage surging from 16.7% (2019) to 81.8% (2026), with several states achieving 100% household connections. A huge infrastructure leap in just a few years.

But the challenge of reliable supply yet to be addressed. Even among “fully covered” states, functionality lags with some states well below 50%.

1 in 6 households (16.9%) report insufficient water quantity. Quality concerns are far lower (7.6% dissatisfied)

India receives $138B annual remittances vs lower FDI inflows, with diaspora income at $700B globally. Remittances nearly doubled in 10 years and finance ~50% of merchandise trade deficit. ~35M diaspora send ~$13,000 every 3 seconds, with >70% expecting stable or higher flows.
